nativeRange.habitat: Mangrove estuaries, tidal creeks, brackish coastal rivers, and freshwater streams; congregates near overhanging vegetation where terrestrial prey can be shot down; also enters fully fresh inland rivers

decorPreferences: overhanging terrestrial plants or branches at the waterline for naturalistic prey-shooting enrichment
robust broadleaf plants (e.g. mangrove propagules, Vallisneria, large Anubias) as shelter
tight-fitting heavy lid — mandatory, strong jumper
low water level below lid to reduce jump-escape gap

diet.notes: Specialist insectivore and surface predator. Wild diet consists of insects, spiders, small lizards, and other terrestrial invertebrates knocked into the water by the archerfish’s famous water-jet spitting. In aquaria, accepts live and frozen crickets, mealworms, waxworms, cockroaches, earthworms, and large frozen bloodworm/krill; may take floating pellets over time. Live prey training is often required for newly imported specimens. Will eat any fish or shrimp small enough to swallow — tankmates must be similar in size. Feed at the surface; drops food to the bottom quickly.

goodWith: other Spottail Archerfish of similar size
large brackish-tolerant fish (Monodactylus spp., Scatophagus argus)
large robust cichlids in freshwater setups
avoidWith: Clouded Archerfish (T. blythii) — competition and hybridisation risk
species requiring stable low salinity or true freshwater only

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Toxotes chatareus showing characteristic dark dorsal spots. Verify availability before use.

summary: The Spottail Archerfish is a large euryhaline predator from SE Asian and Australian mangrove estuaries, famed for shooting jets of water to knock prey from overhanging vegetation. Reaches 30 cm; best kept in brackish setups. Specialist care only.
careNotes:
The Spottail Archerfish (Toxotes chatareus) is the most widely encountered archerfish in the aquarium trade and is frequently marketed as a freshwater species, which is misleading. While juveniles tolerate fully fresh water, adults are significantly healthier and longer-lived in brackish conditions (SG 1.005–1.010+), reflecting their natural mangrove estuary habitat across Southeast Asia and northern Australia. A specific gravity of around 1.005–1.008 is a commonly cited long-term target; pure freshwater is tolerable short-term but linked to increased disease susceptibility and reduced lifespan in reports from experienced keepers.

A tank of at least 400 L with a 150 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a small group of adults. The water surface is this species’ primary domain — they cruise just below the surface scanning for prey. Overhanging decor or a dedicated feeding station positioned above the waterline dramatically enriches captive behaviour and encourages the iconic spitting display. A tight-fitting, heavy lid is non-negotiable: Toxotes chatareus is a powerful and determined jumper, and escapes from uncovered tanks are extremely common.

Keep in groups of three or more; solitary specimens tend to be shy and may refuse food. Conspecific aggression is generally low at equivalent size. Do not mix with any fish small enough to be swallowed. Water quality must be pristine — use strong mechanical filtration and keep nitrates below 20 ppm. Temperature should be maintained at 24–30°C; drops below 22°C cause immune suppression. Newly imported fish often require live prey (crickets, mealworms) before transitioning to frozen equivalents; patience and consistent feeding at the surface are necessary.

Do not confuse with the Clouded Archerfish (T. blythii, fw-fish-302), which is a true freshwater specialist from large Burmese and Thai river systems — T. chatareus is the euryhaline estuarine species.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reliably documented for aquarium-scale setups. In the wild, spawning occurs in estuarine waters; eggs and larvae are pelagic. No reports of captive spawning or fry-rearing in the hobbyist literature exist. Broodstock maintained at public aquaria have occasionally spawned but rearing larvae to juvenile stage has proven highly difficult. Considered not achievable in captivity for practical purposes.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Toxotes chatareus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 30 cm), temperature (24–30°C), pH (7.0–8.0), hardness, native range, brackish requirement (SG 1.005+), diet (insectivore), behaviour, tank size recommendations, and compatibility notes. Confirms euryhaline nature and that freshwater-only keeping is suboptimal long-term.

additional.name: FishBase — Toxotes chatareus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 40 cm reported wild, 30 cm typical aquarium adult), native range (India to Australia), taxonomy (Toxotidae, Perciformes), temperature tolerance (22–28°C), and euryhaline classification. Wild habitat listed as estuaries, mangroves, and freshwater streams.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Archerfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide confirming brackish preference, minimum tank size, lid requirement, insectivore diet, surface-feeding behaviour, and group keeping recommendations. Notes that most store-sold archerfish are T. chatareus.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Archerfish species profile
additional.notes: Detailed species profile distinguishing T. chatareus (euryhaline, estuarine) from T. blythii (freshwater). Confirms brackish SG 1.005–1.010 recommendation, group size of 3+, large tank requirement, and spitting behaviour enrichment with overhanging prey.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Archerfish in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Historical hobbyist reference covering archerfish husbandry. Notes the common trade confusion between freshwater and brackish species, strong recommendation for brackish conditions for T. chatareus, feeding transition strategies from live to frozen prey, and escape-risk warnings.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed captive breeding in aquarium setups. Pelagic eggs and larvae; public-aquarium-scale attempts have not produced viable fry consistently. Not achievable for hobbyist breeding.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Euryhaline brackish-preferring archerfish reaches 30cm. Sold as freshwater but long-term keeping requires brackish (SG 1.005-1.010+). Tight lid mandatory (jumper). Specialist large-tank brackish setup.
